coredata.dc:title: Biosynthesis and characterization of carbon quantum Dots@CuS composite using water hyacinth leaves and its usage in photocatalytic dilapidation of Brilliant Green dye
coredata.dc:creator: Vyas, Yogeshwari
Chundawat, Priyanka
Dharmendra, Dharmendra
Jain, Abhilasha
Punjabi, Pinki B.
Ameta, Chetna
coredata.dc:description: The investigation on the degradation of water-soluble dye Brilliant green was done by using CQDs@CuS. Water-dissolvable, green incandescent CQDs (carbon quantum dots) were fabricated with a high yield by carbonization of water hyacinth (Eichhornia crassipes) weed for the first time at a mild temperature without using any surface activating reagents or salt. The sol-gel method was utilized for the fabrication of copper sulfide (CuS) nanoparticles. An attempt was made to synthesize a composite of CuS NPs with CQDs. The structural and composition determination of synthesized CuS, CQD, and composite (CQDs/CuS) was done by HRTEM, FESEM, XRD, XPS, FTIR, BET, and EDS techniques. TEM and XRD studies are congruent with the amorphous and hexagonal structure of CQDs and CuS, respectively. Various quality parameters such as DO (dissolved oxygen), COD (chemical oxygen demand), conductivity, salinity, and TDS (total dissolved solids) have been measured to observe the extent of degradation of dye. The comparative study has proven that composite (CQDs/CuS) have shown the greatest photocatalytic action for the dilapidation of Brilliant green than CuS NPs only. The probable trail of the dilapidation of dye has also been suggested.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalysis
CQDs
Water hyacinth
CuS
Green chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Band gap study via Tauc plot
Evaluation of photocatalytic act of CuS NPs and CQDs/CuS
Proposed pathway for the dilapidation of BG by CQDs/CuS

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Malaviya National Institute of Technology, Jaipur

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: One of the Author (Chetna Ameta) is grateful to RUSA 2.0 of MHRD, New Delhi, INDIA (F30(16)SPD/RUSA/2016/178 Dated on 31 March, 2020) for financial support.We are grateful to the Department of Physics, ML Sukhadia University for fetching us XRD spectra and PU (CIL and SAIF Chandigarh) for their amazing and quick services like HRTEM, UV–vis, and FESEM. We would like to appreciate IIT Roorkee for offering XPS facilities. We thank MNIT Jaipur for yielding FTIR facilities and CSMCRI Bhavnagar for their BET facility. One of the Author (Chetna Ameta) is grateful to RUSA 2.0 of MHRD, New Delhi, INDIA (F30(16)SPD/RUSA/2016/178 Dated on 31 March, 2020) for financial support.
One of the Author (Chetna Ameta) is grateful to RUSA 2.0 of MHRD, New Delhi, INDIA ( F30(16)SPD/RUSA/2016/178 Dated on 31 March, 2020) for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD spectra of (a) Hexagonal covellite CuS Nanoparticles congruent with JCPDS card no. 00–001-1281 (b) CQDs@CuS composite.
FESEM images of synthesized CQDs/CuS nanocomposite (a) 500 nm. (b) 400 nm resolution.
HRTEM images of synthesized CQDs/CuS nanocomposite (a) 500 nm. (b) 200 nm. (c) 20 nm. (d) SAED pattern at 10 nm.
HRTEM images of pure CQDs. (a) 200 nm. (b) 100 nm. (c) 50 nm. (d) 20 nm. (e) SAED pattern of CQDs at 10 nm.
EDS spectra of CQDs/CuS composite. (a) EDS pattern of all the three elements. (b) Mapping of all the elements of the composite. (c) Mapping of carbon. (d) Mapping of copper. (e) Mapping of sulfur.
FTIR spectra of (a) Synthesized CQDs. (b) CuS NPs. (c) CQDs/CuS nanocomposite.
Tauc plot of (a) CuS (b) CuS@CQDs between (αhv)2 vs hv (energy) in eV.
BET plots of the CQDs@CuS composite (a) Represent the pore volume vs pore width. (b) Desorption-Adsorption isotherm plot of composite. (c) Allocation of pore diameter.
TGA graphs of the composite (a) Display the variation of Weight % with T (temperature) of CQDs/CuS composite. (b) A plot of first derivatives against the temperature of CQDs/CuS.

XPS spectra of the synthesized CQDs@CuS composite (a) XPS spectrum of Cu; two peaks at 929.46 and 949.49 eV are of Cu2+ (2p3/2) and (2p1/2) respectively. (b) XPS spectrum of sulfur display peaks at 160.43 eV and 166.42 eV are of S2−. (c) XPS spectrum of carbon with two strong apexes at 283.90, and 282.09 eV corresponds to sp2 (CC) and sp3 (C–C). (d) Survey spectrum of CQDs@CuS composite.

Experimental study of the decay of the dye using the synthesized nanocomposite (a) Relative investigations of the decay rate for photolysis, adsorption, CuS alone, and CuS/CQDs composite (b) Effect of pH on dye degradation, pH range from 6.0 to 9.0. (c) A consequence of the dose of catalyst varied from 0.01 g to 0.17 g. (d) Results of variation in dye concentration from 20 ppm to 90 ppm.
Bar graph expressing variation in degradation rate for change in concentration of dyes from 20 ppm to 90 ppm.
Effect of Light Intensity on degradation rate, light intensity was varied from 10 to 70 mWcm−2.
Bar graph displaying the change in decay rate for the number of cycles catalyst was employed (four consecutive cycles were performed).
Test of different scavengers (NaCl, K2Cr2O7, IPA, no scavenger) on dilapidation rate.
Kinetic study of the photocatalytic degradation of BG after optimizing all the parameters at pH = 7.5, dose of catalyst = 0.09 g, concentration of dyes = 50 ppm, light intensity = 50 mW/cm−2.
Systematic pathway for synthesis of CQDs.
Represents the synthesis pathway for the production of the CQDs@CuS nanocomposite.
Mechanism pathway of degradation of Brilliant Green dye.
Details of dyes including structure, CI number, and M.W
Various parameters of a water sample analyzed before and later photocatalytic reaction of BG.

head.ce:title: Biosynthesis and characterization of carbon quantum Dots@CuS composite using water hyacinth leaves and its usage in photocatalytic dilapidation of Brilliant Green dye

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Photochemistry Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, University College of Science,M. L. Sukhadia University, Udaipur, 313001, (Raj), India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University College of Science,M. L. Sukhadia University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Photochemistry Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, University College of Science,M. L. Sukhadia University, Udaipur, 313001, (Raj), India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, St. Xavier's College, Mumbai (Maharashtra), India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, St. Xavier's College, Mumbai (Maharashtra), INDIA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The investigation on the degradation of water-soluble dye Brilliant green was done by using CQDs@CuS. Water-dissolvable, green incandescent CQDs (carbon quantum dots) were fabricated with a high yield by carbonization of water hyacinth (Eichhornia crassipes) weed for the first time at a mild temperature without using any surface activating reagents or salt. The sol-gel method was utilized for the fabrication of copper sulfide (CuS) nanoparticles. An attempt was made to synthesize a composite of CuS NPs with CQDs. The structural and composition determination of synthesized CuS, CQD, and composite (CQDs/CuS) was done by HRTEM, FESEM, XRD, XPS, FTIR, BET, and EDS techniques. TEM and XRD studies are congruent with the amorphous and hexagonal structure of CQDs and CuS, respectively. Various quality parameters such as DO (dissolved oxygen), COD (chemical oxygen demand), conductivity, salinity, and TDS (total dissolved solids) have been measured to observe the extent of degradation of dye. The comparative study has proven that composite (CQDs/CuS) have shown the greatest photocatalytic action for the dilapidation of Brilliant green than CuS NPs only. The probable trail of the dilapidation of dye has also been suggested.

ce:section.ce:para: CQDs (Carbon quantum dots) are zero-dimensional particles under 10 nm [1]. CQDs are blooming attention because of their admirable characteristics including environmental-benign, low toxicity, superior conductance, superb luminescence, and better strength. Researchers are utilizing its properties in several fields such as catalysis, sensing, optronics, and even biomedicine. This catalyst can be employed as a photo-catalyst in advanced oxidation techniques which requires the generation of a very responsive free radical which can degrade a wide range of organic pollutants and dyes. These methods are eco-friendly, environmentally benign, and have a scope of broad series of applications. The photocatalyst is utilized in the photocatalysis process; the photocatalyst is the material that accelerates the photo-induced chemical reaction in appropriate surroundings i.e. redox (reducing and oxidizing concurrently). Numerous researchers have utilized ample photocatalysts to debase the industrial effluent having organic contaminants. Mostly metal oxides were used as a photocatalyst such as TiO2, SnO2, ZnO, Bi2WO6, ZnS [2–6]. Though TiO2 has been used as a photocatalyst it was found that CuS could be a suitable substitute as it has an adequate band gap (2.0 eV), is environment-friendly, and cheap [7]; which can bring to the visible range and utilized for the photocatalysis. Photocatalysis utilized solar radiation, which is a renewable resource of energy without the need for any expensive instrument. Besides current examination has confirmed CuS nanoparticles are assuring photocatalyst for degradation of organic contaminants [8,9]. Photocatalytic efficacy of only CuS is poor enough to entirely debase the organic toxin. Therefore the realistic function is still difficult to produce a better result.

CQDs can be prepared by two approaches bottom-up or top-down. In the top-down technique, the bulk material is sliced down to tiny QDs. Laser ablation and electrochemical etching come under the category of a top down approach. Cui et al. [10] utilize carbon cloth for the production of CQDs using the laser beam technique. Pure water was used as a precursor for the fabrication of CQDs via electrochemical etching by Ming et al. [11].

In bottom-up technique involves the solvothermal, sol-gel, MW exposure, and carbonization method. The solvothermal method employs an autoclave operated at high pressure and high-temperature solvent to synthesize CQDs. Elango et al. [12] employed wastage of crab shell for the formation of CQDs by the hydrothermal method in a step. Enhanced microwave irradiation was applied on fenugreek seeds to obtain CQDs [13]. Carbonization is recently one of the favorite methods of researchers since it has plenty of advantages like green routes, lesser reagents, and low temperature. Carbonization includes heating the carbon-based material and reaching the desired QDs using appropriate reagents. Environmental friendly precursors are more widely employed such as biomass [14], aloe vera [15], pomegranate [16], and banana [17].

Carbon quantum dots can be employed in diverse function like bio-imaging [18,19], drug delivery [20], photocatalysis [21], sensors [22], and optronics [23]. Nowadays Water parting is at boom and CQDs are environmentally benign material highly used for the parting of water and simultaneously liberation of H2. CQDs were used as co-catalyst in Photo-electrochemical water parting by Wang et al. [24]. CQDs have the potential application since has electron transfer property under the influence of sunlight helps them to act as both electron giver and taker along with up-and down-conversion PL (photoluminescence) [25,26]. The PL nature of CQDs can be attuned by the method of fabrication since they are strongly correlated with synthesis procedure, reaction conditions like time, temperature, solvent, and initial material, modification, or functionalization of the surface. The up-conversion and PL features of CQDs make it an apt candidate for ample application. Hence CQDs possesses unique properties, therefore, can act as a catalyst, co-catalyst, or carrier catalyst in numerous application including catalysis, sensors, storage, and energy exchange [27].

Due to these properties, CQDs can act as supporting catalyst which is proficient in operating a wider range of near-infrared (NIR) or visible light to amplify the action of the chief photocatalyst [28]. Some studies showing decoration of metal oxides on carbon quantum dots (e.g. CQDs/CuO [16], Fe2O3/CQDs [29], and CQD/TiO2 [30]) have shown excellent photocatalytic activity in recent research. Based on the literature survey, it was prophesied that the composite of CuS and CQDs could be employed to improve the photocatalytic dilapidation of organic dyes and contaminants under observable light. Within this framework, the CQDs have been prepared via weed (Water hyacinth leaves) via green synthesis and copper sulfide nanoparticles (CuS NPs) via the sol-gel method. The composite of CQDs/CuS was synthesized using a Microwave and ultrasonicator thus, exhibiting extra photocatalytic proficiency for dilapidation of Brilliant Green (BG) under visible light. The internal structure and surface studies of composite (CQDs/CuS) were carefully discussed. A Spectrophotometer was used for investigating the kinetics of dilapidation of the reaction. Also proposed trail for the dilapidation of Brilliant green dye.

The significance of this work is that it is not only eliminating the toxic materials from the environment it is eliminating poison from a poison i.e., weed. Hence cleaning the environment with the weed and by this way, two toxins are reduced from the environment utilizing easy technique without any hazardous chemicals or costly instrument. The novel CQDs/CuS is prepared for the first time using a greener method hence we have followed the principles of green chemistry which is crucial for a sustainable environment and human health. The importance of this scheme is a dual benefit since toxin was eliminated i.e., BG dye using a hazardous weed i.e., water hyacinth. Hence this scheme solves two problems simultaneously which are double beneficial to nature and human health. The process is cheap since it utilized the green technique, no hazardous solvent is utilized. It is the best technique, which can be utilized on large scale for the purification of industrial effluents as it need only solar energy which is a renewable source of energy and novel photocatalyst. It is dual removal of pollutants which is eco-friendly, cheap, and follows greener routes.
Materials and photocatalytic experimental details are elaborated in the supplementary information (SI).

The schematic representation of the synthesis of CQDs via green synthesis is illustrated in Scheme 1 . Water hyacinths (Eichhornia crassipes) leaves were obtained from Pichola Lake, Udaipur, Rajasthan. Water hyacinth leaves (WHL) were collected, washed, and dried in sunlight by covering them with a cotton cloth for 14 days. Leaves were dried, all water has been evaporated, and then the dried WHL was ground using a mixer grinder in the kitchen. After grinding, for carbonization oven drying (hot air oven) has been done at 150 °C for 15 h. 6 g of the WHL powder (invasive material) was washed with 180 mL of an aqueous HNO3 solution (0.1 M) and rinsed with distilled water followed by absolute alcohol. The obtained product is dried at ambient conditions for 18 h. Then above powder is mixed with sodium hypochlorite solution (100 mL) and left at an ambient situation for 12 h. This solution is sieved and cleaned with distilled water (DW) till the filtrate becomes neutral. The oxidized WHL was mixed with 150 mL of water and placed in a hot air oven at 140 °C and gradually increase up to 160 °C for 18 h. The obtained coffee color compound was washed to eliminate the unreacted organic species by dichloromethane. To this distilled water is added and aged (kept undisturbed) for 48 h then supernatant is separated using a syringe (20 mL). This process is repeated till the color of supernatant water is clear and transparent. Then the coffee color solution is centrifuged (inside the centrifuge at 240 V and 50 Hz) and washed. The final CQDs solution in ethanol was preserved at 4 °C (inside the refrigerator) for further use.

CuS nanoparticles (NPs) were prepared using a sol-gel method using copper (II) chloride dihydrate as a precursor and sodium sulfide as a reagent. The desired amount of copper precursor (CuCl2. 2H2O) was dissolved in an adequate amount of water. After Ultrasonic treatment at room temperature for 30 min, Na2S (0.1 M) solution is added. After 30 min, the precipitate of CuS NPs was filtered, washed, and dried.

0.2 M alcoholic solution of CQDs is additionally taken in reactant otherwise method is similar as above (synthesis of CuS NPs). Scheme 2 represents the synthesis pathway for the production of the CQDs@CuS nanocomposite.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of CuS nanoparticles and CQDs/CuS nanocomposite were recorded using XPERT-PRO diffractometer by Cu Kα radiation at 45 kV and 40 MA with a scanning rate of 4° per minute. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were spotted in ATR mode on the PerkinElmer spectrophotometer. The band gap of nanomaterial was determined by UV–Vis absorption spectra obtained from UV–Vis spectrophotometer Model Lambda 750 (PerkinElmer). Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) image was applied on TESCAN equipped with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy at 10 kV accelerating voltage, Model JSM6100 (Jeol) with Image Analyser. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was achieved by TEM Hitachi (H-7500). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out using Al Kα (1486.6 eV) radiation and an Ultraviolet source. Pore diameter, pore size, and surface analysis have been done by the BET technique. The UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded on Systronics 2371 spectrophotometer using a glass cuvette of volume 4 mL.
The XRD pattern of CuS NPs and composite (CQDs@CuS) is shown in Fig. 1 a and b, respectively.

Fig. 1a displays 2θ (o) positioned at 27.84° (101), 29.47° (102), 32.33° (103), 33.11° (006), 48.20° (110), 52.93° (114), 59.47° (116) and 74.81° (208), which congruent with card no. 00–001-1281 (JCPDS). No other peaks were observed which confirms that generated NPs were crystalline and pure possessing lattice parameters a = 3.7901 (Å), b = 3.7901 (Å), and c = 16.2800 (Å). The mean size of CuS NPs is 12.49 nm from the following formula.
D =
0.9
λ
β cos θ
Here D is crystalline size, β is line broadening in radians, θ represents Bragg angle, and λ is the wavelength of X-Ray.

Fig. 1b composite XRD display peak position (2θ) at 18.80, 28.78, 32.28, 35.33, 48.03, 49.94, 59.14 which is similar to CuS; only extra peak at 18.80 is due to (002) plane of CQDs. The mean size of the composite is 4.39 nm. XRD peaks confirm the highly crystalline and hexagonal covellite nature of CuS NPs. The prominent and spiky peaks of CuS prove the hexagonal covellite nature of CuS [31].
Fig. 2 shows the FESEM pictures of the composite (CQDs/CuS), displays the spherical-shaped composite.

Many tiny spheres (average size = 15.32 nm) are present. Composite (CQDs/CuS) illustrates the complete agglomeration of all the particles CQDs and CuS. FESEM confirms the successful synthesis of CQDs/CuS composite. The typical diameter of CQDs/CuS nanoparticles was found to be less than 20 nm and the composites look like globular patterns one over the other with a certain extent of agglomeration but are distinctive. Particle distribution is shown in S1.
HRTEM images of CQDs/CuS composite are displayed in Fig. 3 , which displays compact loading of Carbon dots over CuS NPs.

HRTEM studies demonstrate the compact growth of CQDs all over the CuS and composite have nearly spherical shapes. The produced CQDs/CuS composite was 15–25 nm in diameter. Fig. 3d displays the SAED image; it reveals the polycrystalline characteristics of the composite with 0.446 nm interplanar distance (Fig. S2). The HRTEM of CQDs displays a circular shape (Fig. 4 ).

The size of CQDs comes around 4–8 nm and with 4.66 nm interplanar distance (Fig. S3). Fig. 4e exhibits the SAED image, where the lack of the diffraction point and occurrence of continuous rings indicates the amorphous nature of CQDs which agrees with XRD analysis.

The elemental composition of synthesized photocatalyst (CQDs/CuS) was determined by EDS spectroscopy. EDS pattern as shown in Fig. 5 a confirms the occurrence of all the elements including copper, sulfur, and carbon. Fig. 5b shows the mapping of all elements which confirms perfect composite formation. Hence, an accepted form of CQDs/CuS has been synthesized.
FTIR spectrum of CQDs, CuS, and CQDs/CuS is presented in Fig. 6 a, b, and 6c, correspondingly.

The FT-IR spectra of CQDs have shown in Fig. 6a, where a –OH stretching band seen at 3453 cm−1, band corresponds to 2994 cm−1 is of C–H stretching, bending mode of O–H appears at 1655.1 cm−1 and a peak near 1445.7 cm−1 designated to the vibration (stretching) of aromatic (CC) carbon. C–O bond vibrations can be identified by the 1066.87 cm−1 band and the epoxy group peak appears at 842.63 cm−1.

The FTIR spectra of CuS nanoparticles shown in Fig. 6b; display two wide bands at 3420.99 cm−1 due to –OH group elongation and 2844.91 cm−1 peak is stretching frequency (C–H). The acme at 556.50 cm−1 denotes Cu–S stretching modes [32]. A prickly peak at 1624.14 cm−1 is of bending mode of water [33]. A peak at 1205.0 cm−1 is designated to stretch (asymmetric) of CO bonded group.

An FTIR spectrum of CQDs@CuS is displayed in Fig. 6c. It has all the peaks of both CuS and C-Quantum dots. Various peaks are observed at 3421.02, 1689.30, 1595.35, 1520.03, 1345.99, 1107.08, 841.52 and 551.11 cm−1. Slight shifting in frequency indicates the triumphant creation of composite. The peak between 1300 and 1600 cm−1 is the stretching frequency of alkene moiety and the 750-950 cm−1 peak corresponds to the bending of the CC bond of an alkene. Hence FTIR validates the formation of CQDs/CuS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Band gap study via Tauc plot
ce:section.ce:para: Absorption spectra have been recorded out (200–800 nm range) using a UV–vis spectrophotometer. Fig. 7 represents the Tauc plot ((αhv)2 vs hv).

It exposed that the band gap of CQDs/CuS composite was spotted at 2.96 eV and of CuS alone was near 3.46 eV. The reduction in band gap might be possible to the interstitial defect that arises by the inclusion of CQDs. The lesser value of nanocomposite (CQDs/CuS) contrasted to pure CuS NPs authenticated the superior absorption capacity of composite [34]. Thus, it can be employed in visible light photocatalytic reactions.

The surface area of photocatalyst is directly related to photocatalytic proficiency, therefore BET study is fruitful. Fig. 8 exhibited the isotherm (desorption-adsorption). The surface area of CuS NPs as determined by BET studies was reported to be 9.56 m2/g [35] whereas the surface area of CQDs/CuS was found to be 15.4219 m2/g by BET examination; which is larger thus enhances the photocatalytic activity. The rise in surface area is due to the evolution of composite. The volume of the composite and pore size are 0.643751 cm3/g and 1669.708 A°, respectively. BJH technique was employed which yields 14.158 as average pore diameter. The rectilinear surface area graph of the composite is shown in Fig. S4. BET investigation confirms that the composite is an efficient photocatalyst and able to degrade pollutants to a greater extent.

Weight fall of 4.57% is reported due to adsorbed water present on the sample till the temperature was below 200 °C, (Fig. 9 a). Fig. 9b portrayed this descend corresponds to the elimination of H2O from the sample (CQDs@CuS). The decline of weight (−4.57%) in the temperature section from 200 °C to 315 °C ascribed to the conversion of CuS to Cu2S (−13.93) [36]. After 315 °C–621 °C, Cu2S (+26.1%) changes to CuSO4. From 621 to 842 °C, it decomposes to CuO (33.81%). DSC analysis of the sample (CQDs@CuS) is presented in S5.
XPS was done to know the chemical constitution of the CQDs/CuS (composite) is given in Fig. 10 .

Fig. 10d illustrates the complete XPS spectrum of CQDs@CuS. The subsistence of carbon (C 1s), copper (Cu 2p), and sulfur (S 2p) were proved. The specific elemental spectrum of all the three-element is displayed in Fig. 10a, b, and 10c. A diminished Gaussian peak of Cu (2p) was observed at 941.11 eV corresponding to satellite peak [37]. (Fig. 10a). Apex located at 929.46 owns minimum B.E. (binding energy) parallel with Cu2+ (2p3/2) and another at 949.49 associated with Cu2+ (2p1/2). The apexes matching up to CuS are similar to the reported literature data [38]. The distance across two Cu apexes is roughly 20.03 eV which is recognized as the standard value of Cu. Subordinate apexes were positioned at elevated energy than the foremost tip of Cu 2p3/2 [39]. Hence spectrum reiterates the +2 O.S. (oxidation state) of Cu and eradicates the probability of +1 O.S.

Figure 10b (S 2p) demonstrates three broad deconvoluted apexes indicated using varied colours. The red colour acme at 159.44 eV (low energy) expresses the amount of sulfur in the CQDs@CuS composite, which corresponds to the copper-sulfur bond. The two remaining acmes positioned at 160.43 eV and 166.42 eV are of S2− state [40]. XPS (High-resolution) of C 1s is given in Fig. 10c which reveals three deconvoluted apexes at 282.16, 282.09, and 283.90 eV. The two strong apexes at 283.90, and 282.09 eV corresponds to sp2 (CC) and sp3 (C–C) carbon, correspondingly [41]. All the examinations of composite validate the successful synthesis of composite i.e., CQDs@CuS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of photocatalytic act of CuS NPs and CQDs/CuS
ce:section.ce:para: Brilliant green (BG) dye was chosen as a subject compound to showcase the photocatalytic performance of produced CQDs/CuS composite. Brilliant green (BG) is highly venomous. The detail of the dye including MW and CI number is given in Table 1 . This dye is usually used in biological and paper industries for tint. Also used in poultry feed to avoid fungi and parasite contagion. This dye badly affects the respiratory tract, particularly the lungs and also causes skin and eye irritation. Gastric and respiratory tracts are seriously affected by contact and gasping. The greatest wavelength absorbance of Brilliant green was found at 625 nm.

CQDs/CuS catalyst degrades the 96% dye in 90 min beneath visible light introduction. Equivalent experiments were carried out without catalyst and in dark to inspect the outcome of light and catalyst on the degradation response. The photolysis remained inept for the dye deterioration in absence of a catalyst. Additionally, the photocatalytic dilapidation of Brilliant green dye was executed with unadulterated CuS NPs and CQDs/CuS. The comparative study demonstrated that only 38% of the dye was depleted in 90 min under observable light introduction in presence of CuS NPs, whereas 96% degradation was reported for CQDs/CuS under similar experimental situations (Fig. 11 a). Thus, it can be concluded that the CQDs/CuS demonstrate higher photocatalytic efficiency for the degradation of Brilliant green, in which produced Carbon QDs plays a vital role to enhance decay rate.
Various parameters including pH of the solution, quantity of catalyst, the inceptive concentration of dye, and intensity of light were also optimized, which rules the efficacy of the composite.

Firstly, the solution's pH was adjusted maintaining other bounds constantly. The investigations were made in a basic and acidic medium which discloses that there is better degradation at near-neutral region so pH range from 6 to 9 was studied for the adjustment of pH (Fig. 11b). The optimized pH was established to be 7.5.

To evaluate an adequate dose of nano-catalyst for the decay process of BG photo catalytically, the amount of CQDs@CuS was varied from 0.01 to 0.17 g keeping 7.5 pH and 50 ppm of contaminant concentration (Fig. 11c). It exposed that by varying the quantity of nano-catalyst (0.01–0.09 g), the decomposition rate of BG pollutant was improved from 23.7% to 91.8%, since the advancement of convenience on vigorous spots on the nanocatalysts surface. At 0.17 g of catalyst, the fall goes to 38.3%. The reason behind the fall is the turbidity caused by a high amount which restricts the path of radicals to the substrate. Hence 0.09 g is the suitable quantity of composite.

The pollutant dye concentration was varied from 20 to 90 ppm keeping other parameters constant (Fig. 11d). It was discovered that as the pollutant concentration rises from 20 ppm to 50 ppm, the degradation improved from 57.9% to 87.8%, and with additional enhancement in concentration up to 90 ppm the disintegration cut down to 39.1%. Initially, the enhancement can be ascribed to more availability of dye molecules but further enhancement causes retardation in rate because of more collision amongst the molecules of dyes than with the composite. Furthermore, the contaminant molecules get fixed on the nanocatalyst's surface hence the surface is unavailable for interaction. Fig. 12 represents the bar graph of degradation rate vs. dye concentration.

Upshot of light exposure on dilapidation was also deliberated and optimized. For this, the range of 10–70 mW/cm−2 intensity was examined. Fig. 13 depicts the improvement in the rate of degradation as the intensity of light enhances and the highest rate was obtained for 50 mW/cm−2. Increasing light strength enhances the number of photons hitting per unit area leads to a higher rate of degradation. Afterward, the rate declined due to sites remaining unvarying although the intensity is amplified [42].

Reusability is checked by multiple uses of catalysts. For this catalyst was carefully recovered from the reaction after the use by washing and drying. Fig. 14 displays the observed rate for sequential cycles (96.36%, 96.32%, 96.14% and 95.27%). A minor fall in rate after each use, demonstrate the produced nanocatalyst unveiled high photocatalytic efficiency for the BG dilapidation.

Characteristics of energetic radicals were found out by using the scavengers test. Fig. 15 displays the effect of different scavengers on the decay process. IPA, K2Cr2O7, and NaCl were utilized as scavengers for OH radical, electron, and holes respectively. The decay rate was 96.3% in devoid of scavengers and then it falls to 89.1%, 49.8%, and 52.9% in the presence of NaCl, K2Cr2O7, and IPA, respectively.

The reports divulge that h+ and OH are monitoring the principal reactive species in the photocatalytic dilapidation of BG. Kinetics of photocatalytic reaction was examined by Langmuir- Hinshelwood model,
ln
(
C 0
/
C t
)
= k . t

C0 represents the concentration at the t = 0 and Ct is the concluding concentration of Brilliant Green time reaches t and k = rate-constant. The study reports the pseudo-first-order kinetics for photocatalytic decay of BG dye. Rate-constant was deduced to 0.036 min−1 as can be seen in Fig. 16 using slope.

The water standard of the contaminant water sample was analyzed before and after setting up the degradation reaction by measuring various parameters as presented in Table 2 . Enhancement observed for conductance, DO, TDS and salinity and Decrement in pH and COD which supports the complete dilapidation of dye.
The photocatalytic competence of CQDs/CuS towards BG (a triarylmethane dye) was examined beneath perceptible light illumination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed pathway for the dilapidation of BG by CQDs/CuS
ce:section.ce:para: The superior performance of CQDs/CuS validated the eminence of CQDs in the photocatalytic reaction. Therefore, CQDs/CuS demonstrates more absorption of light because of quantum dots in the composite. CQDs helped CuS NPs to effectively produce electrons e−) and holes (h+). Further CQDs help to stop the reunification of e− and h+ since they can act as acceptors and providers of electrons which improves the effectiveness of CQDs/CuS composite. Thus, better separation proficiency advances the duration of the photogenerated charge transporters by blockading the rejoining of h+ and e−, providing many charge carriers to give greatly responsive species and extensively enlarge the photocatalytic performance of the CQDs/CuS [43]. Upon illumination, the electrons reached the conduction band (CB) from the valence band (VB) of CuS, and these electrons are rearranged in CQDs. Hence yielding a vacancy in the VB and thus restraining the holes and electrons rejoining.

Location of band edge of CQDs/CuS was calculated using the equation written below
EVB = Xe – Ee + 0.5 Eg
ECB = EVB – Eg

EVB and ECB represent the valence and conduction band edge potentials of CQDs/CuS, Ee (4.5 eV) denotes the free energy of e−, Xe displays electronegativity and Eg (2.96 eV) is the band gap of CQDs/CuS. The position of band EVB and ECB were at 3.27 eV and 0.69 eV, respectively. EVB of CQDs/CuS (3.27 eV) is higher than the calibre potential (redox) .OH to OH anion, (1.99 eV vs NHE), accordingly possess the more oxidizing ability. The OH radicals were generated by the reaction of photoinduced electrons with oxygen on the periphery of CQDs. OH radical is produced by the reaction of holes with water in light existence. These extremely responsive species are capable of the dilapidation of organic toxins competently. The proposed mechanism of the degradation process is represented in Scheme 3 .

Bio-based green luminescent carbon quantum dots with a better yield were designed via a weed water hyacinth (Eichhornia crassipes) via a greener and direct method exclusive of any special reagent or apparatus. The Copper sulfide (CuS) nanoparticles were fabricated using the sol-gel technique. Comparative studies showed that the composite of CuS NPs with CQDs owns the highest photocatalytic action on the dilapidation of Brilliant green than CuS NPs only. Results of all the studies including FTIR, XRD, HRTEM, FESEM, BET, and XPS concordant and proves the successful creation of composite. All these studies have proven the formation of efficient photocatalyst which can be employed for the dilapidation of a wide range of organic contaminants.
One of the Author (Chetna Ameta) is grateful to RUSA 2.0 of MHRD, New Delhi, INDIA (F30(16)SPD/RUSA/2016/178 Dated on 31 March, 2020) for financial support.

Yogeshwari Vyas: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation, Investigation. Priyanka Chundawat: Software. Dharmendra Dharmendra: Software. Abhilasha Jain: Review. Pinki B. Punjabi: Validation. Chetna Ameta: Conceptualization, Visualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful to the Department of Physics, ML Sukhadia University for fetching us XRD spectra and PU (CIL and SAIF Chandigarh) for their amazing and quick services like HRTEM, UV–vis, and FESEM. We would like to appreciate IIT Roorkee for offering XPS facilities. We thank MNIT Jaipur for yielding FTIR facilities and CSMCRI Bhavnagar for their BET facility. One of the Author (Chetna Ameta) is grateful to RUSA 2.0 of MHRD, New Delhi, INDIA (F30(16)SPD/RUSA/2016/178 Dated on 31 March, 2020) for financial support.
